tweets.text:
Vlad on why equity multiples should come down across the board because of AI.

He explains why companies like Salesforce can be used by nearly every Fortune 500 company in perpetuity and still face a major valuation headwind.

"I can't find a time in my career, or in history, where it's not macro conditions driving the repricing of equities and credit, but pure uncertainty about how this technology will evolve and change the fabric of the economy.

Past disruptions were a slow burn. This is different.

You're literally saying the future is so unpredictable that how could I possibly place some higher multiple on something.

You'll have trillion dollar companies in 2030 that don't exist today, and trillion dollar companies now that won't exist.

The first question people asked was whether everyone would vibe code their own CRM.

Of course not, that's absurd. That's not the question.

The real question is whether the trillion dollar company that's still just an idea somewhere today will ever use Salesforce.

If the answer is no, that's a massive headwind to the valuation, even if every Fortune 500 company uses it into perpetuity.

It's a cash cow, and a cash cow deserves a different multiple."

tweets.text:
Vlad grew up in Soviet Moldova and describes what makes America special:

"When you're born outside the United States, you're exposed to a way of life that's very difficult to understand for those fortunate enough to be born here.

You take certain things for granted.

You assume they're like gravity, because they just exist. But they're not.

What is true in the U.S. — always has been true, still true today — is that the level of agency you have is unparalleled anywhere else in the world.

If you have talents and motivation, there's an infinite number of ways to define success and live a life where you have the option to thrive.

That option is not available to the vast majority of humanity.

You'll be burdened by your family's history, your ethnicity, your religion, your government's oppressive system.

In Moldova, you're not given permission to dream. You're born to survive.

When you come to the U.S., you experience literally the inverse of that — this notion of individualism.

In the U.S., you have an opportunity.

The U.S. frees a person to pursue their talents and interests in a way that's just impossible elsewhere."

tweets.text:
Vlad Barbalat holds one of the most interesting seats in the investment world.

He manages the $120 billion permanent capital platform within Liberty Mutual with the flexibility to access nearly any opportunity in any form.

One of my favorite parts of talking to Vlad is how he sees America. 

He grew up in Soviet Moldova, where his family was persecuted, and his love for this country comes from knowing what another system can do to people.

His story is one of the best reminders that none of us should take America for granted.

We discuss:
- How AI may force lower equity multiples 
- What Liberty looks for in a new GP or deal
- The benefits and downsides of permanent capital
- What makes Liberty one of the most sought after LPs
- Why the people who love America most are often immigrants

Enjoy!

tweets.text:
3 major board members are leaving @SonicLabs:

→ Andre Cronje
→ Michael Kong
→ David Richardson

And while the resignations are unfortunate, the timing matters even more because this is happening while price, TVL, and sentiment are already weak ( $S is trading about 97% below its ATH ).

Feelsbadman, but I don’t think this means Sonic is dead.

The engineering team is still active and Sonic said around 400 meaningful code updates were merged in 2026, so they are def still kicking from the dev side.

However, it does mean Sonic now has to rebuild confidence without its strongest public technical figure: Andre Cronje.

I’m curious to see the rebound here, TBH, this is usually the part of the market where winners are made anyway.

We recently had the first NASDAQ listing for one of our portfolio companies, which was a great success.

However, the whole process just reminded me once again how broken the TradFi world truly is and by how many orders of magnitude crypto rails could make all of this better.

Even though the listing was over a month ago, half a dozen middlemen and a very opaque share transfer process have resulted in us still not having our shares to this day.

And the funniest part: no matter which of these middlemen you talk to, nobody knows what's going on and nobody is at fault.

Whenever I have to deal with banks or institutional players like this, it just reminds me every single time how inefficient and intransparent the TradFi world is, running on rails from 20 to 30 years ago, and how painfully early but also inevitable the crypto industry is.

I don't care about bear or bull markets or what kind of problems we have yet to solve to make it happen. Nothing has changed about the revolutionary vision of crypto and sooner or later it's going to happen.

With or without you.

@CleanTechCTV $CTV.V $CTVFF #uranium #nuclear #fluorspar

Everyone is talking about AI.

Few are talking about what powers AI.

AI data centers require enormous amounts of electricity. That's one reason governments and utilities are increasingly turning toward nuclear energy.

Here's the overlooked part:

Before uranium can be enriched, it must first be converted into uranium hexafluoride (UF₆).

1 uranium atom + 6 fluorine atoms = UF₆

No UF₆ = no enrichment.

And fluorspar is the world's primary commercial source of fluorine.

AI → Electricity → Nuclear → Fluorspar

As AI drives power demand higher, the importance of secure domestic fluorspar supply may become increasingly difficult to ignore.

CleanTech $CTV.V is developing projects in the historic Illinois-Kentucky Fluorspar District, which historically supplied most U.S. fluorspar production.

Drill program coming up. Website refreshed. Worth a look: https://t.co/8QJEaEPi0F

Disclosure: Paid partnership with CleanTech Vanadium. Not financial advice. Always do your own due diligence.

tweets.text:
This year, the EF is decreasing its budget by roughly 40%, which entails some difficult decisions. The goal of the decreases was set out in the Treasury Management Policy last year: the EF is transitioning into being a long-term-oriented endowment-based organization, shifting from its pre-2026 average of spending ~15% of its remaining funds each year, toward a post-2030 target of ~5% per year.

Often, when an organization goes through something like this, people try to pretend that nothing of great value was lost, that it is an efficiency increase, that the only people cut are unproductive dead weight, and everyone else stopped partying, studied the blade, entered cracked S-tier beast mode, and this was sufficient to make up for the downside. I will not try to pretend this. I respect my EF colleagues far too much to pretend that there was not much that is lost. They are brilliant people. They are dedicated engineers of whom some have worked on the Ethereum protocol for nearly a decade. They have brought a bright light to the Ethereum ecosystem with their code, their words, their warmth as human beings and their actions. My dearest hope is that they find a path that brings them fulfillment and happiness whether inside Ethereum or outside. Hopefully many will be able to bring their excellent talents and mindset to the wider Ethereum ecosystem, or the even wider CROPS world.

Instead, I will try to explain what *are* some of the grand sacrifices being made. The Ethereum Strawmap is no small thing. It is an extremely ambitious undertaking seeking to replace and augment almost every part of the protocol - consensus, proofs, privacy, account model, state, and more. This is the third iteration of Ethereum, in the same way that the Merge was the second, even if the shipping style is less Big Bang and more one-piece-at-a-time. On top of this, the EF is increasing its role in the Access Layer. We are not compromising on Ethereum being a Deeply Impressive protocol, something worthy of its place in a world with quantum computing, rockets to Mars and powerful biotech and AI, and capable of meeting the challenges that this era will bring.

Some of the deficit will be recovered through more work happening outside the EF. But not all. So what are the grand sacrifices that will enable a leaner effort to accomplish all of this? I will give a few examples (though far from an exhaustive list):

* The multi-client model will shift in the direction of multiple clients existing less for _redundancy_, and more for _specialization_. Up to this point, redundancy has been the main security strategy: if one client has a bug, if it has less than 33%, the chain keeps going and does not even stop finalizing. We are increasingly exploring moving more pieces of the protocol to a different security strategy: AI-assisted formal verification. Some smaller pieces of Ethereum (eg. BLS libraries) have worked this way already for a long time. But soon many more parts of Ethereum will likely function on this model. This may greatly reduce resource requirements of shipping a large number of EIPs. The resources saved by client teams can ideally instead be used to better serve different specialized user needs, including EF Access Layer goals.
* PSE (Privacy and Scaling Explorations) is winding down as a unit. The number of people working on ZKPs for privacy and scaling is probably as high as ever, but they are working less on "exploration" and more on *implementing* ZKP-based privacy and scaling into the Protocol and Access Layer
* Devcon will likely over time become smaller-scale, somewhat more spartan, much lower-deficit than previous years, in addition to other changes in vision in line with the Mandate.
* Fewer beyond-Ethereum megaprojects coming from EF. As I announced earlier this year, I am taking on some of the responsibility of doing projects in this category that I consider valuable with my personal funds.
* EF institutional work is reducing in scope, specializing more specifically on creating replicable test cases of highly CROPS-friendly deployments, even if at smaller scale.

These do not explain all departures; in some cases they do not explain departures at all and rather explain _reduced need for new spending_. But they are a large part of the strategy at play.

In the longer term, I personally favor a "soft lean-and-done" approach to Ethereum: once the Strawmap is completed, generally stick to security fixes and small high-value changes, and have a much higher bar for considering new feature additions to the protocol. This allows Ethereum to remain capture-resistant without demanding very large budgets. Learn less from multimillion-line-of-code behemoth projects, more from bitcoin.

The past years have been a challenging era for Ethereum. However, the ecosystem is adapting, both inside the EF and outside, and I am confident that Ethereum is very well-positioned to succeed and thrive.
 https://t.co/iZiOonRYzR

tweets.text:
Tokenizing credit doesn’t make it inherently better
Bad loan is a bad loan doesn’t matter if it’s tokenized
Still need to underwrite quality!  
Sad to see another failure of credit onchain but I still think the industry will grow. Areas of opportunity:

> fixed rate & longer term
> quality credit (sponsor / vault manager having skin in the game to overcome principal agent problem)

tweets.text:
𝗜𝘀𝗿𝗮𝗲𝗹’𝘀 𝗦𝘁𝗼𝗰𝗸 𝗠𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁 𝗛𝗮𝘀 𝗗𝗼𝘂𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗱. 𝗛𝗮𝘀 𝗔𝗻𝘆𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝗡𝗼𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗥𝗶𝘀𝗸𝘀?

The Israeli market has roughly doubled in the last three years. Despite a recent pullback of around 10% from the highs, it remains strongly positive year-to-date. The problem is that the market seems to be pricing perfection at a time when the risks keep piling up.

The war with Iran ended in the worst possible way for Israel, Iran still remains an existential threat. Netanyahu promised to stop Iran. He didn’t. The problem wasn’t solved. It was postponed.

Now that Iran is off the front page, Netanyahu seems ready to go back to what he was doing before October 7th, destroying the system: fighting the judiciary, fighting institutions, and deepening divisions inside Israel.

His coalition, heavily dependent on nationalist and religious-right parties, appears far more focused on reshaping Israel’s institutions than addressing the country’s long-term economic and demographic challenges.

Real estate is another story. After one of the biggest housing booms in the developed world, cracks are starting to appear: rising inventory, slower sales, developers offering increasingly aggressive financing deals, affordability near record lows and weak rental yields.

Then there is tech.

A huge part of Israel’s economy is built on a relatively small group of highly productive people: software developers, engineers, entrepreneurs, doctors, financiers and business owners.

That engine is slowing.

Layoffs have been happening for years. Venture capital is nowhere near peak levels. Hiring has weakened. AI is increasingly becoming a headwind for many of the jobs Israel built its economy around.

Then there is the demographic issue nobody wants to discuss.

The fastest-growing part of the population is also the part with the lowest workforce participation rates. More people depend on the productive economy while a smaller percentage carries it.

And then there is the shekel.

The shekel has strengthened dramatically and is trading as if Israel’s long-term outlook has become substantially safer and stronger.

Maybe it has.

Or maybe the currency is reflecting peak optimism at exactly the wrong time.

If this thesis is correct, investors could eventually be hit from both directions: falling asset prices and a weaker currency.

To be fair, Israel still has major strengths. Natural gas exports. Cybersecurity. A world-class defense industry. A culture of entrepreneurship that is almost impossible to kill.

But markets don’t get into trouble because they ignore the strengths.

They get into trouble because they ignore the risks. Which brings us to the obvious question:

What is the catalyst?
Markets can ignore risks for years. A risk is not a catalyst.

In my view, the most likely catalyst is the next election.

If Netanyahu wins another election and returns with an even stronger coalition dominated by nationalist and religious-right parties, investors could suddenly be forced to reconsider risks that are currently being ignored.

Judicial reform could return to the center of the agenda. The conflict between the government and state institutions could intensify. Political polarization could deepen. Capital that returned to Israel after the Iran war could once again start looking for the exit.

Whether you agree or disagree with the reforms is irrelevant.

Markets care about one thing: certainty.

The moment investors start questioning the long-term stability and predictability of a country’s institutions, risk premiums rise, currencies weaken and asset valuations adjust.

Less trust. Less investment. More capital leaving. Higher taxes. Higher defense spending. Slower growth. Lower asset prices. A weaker shekel.

The Trade: Short TA-90
